speaker: interviewer
text: --with Judith Goldstein. And this is tape number two. My name is Alice Brown. And this is [INAUDIBLE] New York, June 30th, 1995. Tell me, Ms. Goldstein, you were talking about the school. Was that in a special place that was established for this? The Germans gave you a place where you can have that school? Or how did it work?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans didn't give us. The handful of Jews, and I guess the intellectuals and the educators of the ghetto decided that they would create something special in-- in the ghetto. And so they had a conservatory of music. They had a theater. They opened up some schools, including the conservatory. And I remember being involved, not only in the conservatory, but I remember actually meeting Mr. Durmashkin, who happened to be-- whose sister was friends with my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this Mr. Durmashkin?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a conductor. But he also conducted the Hebrew choir. And once I used to sit on the stage in back just to listen to him conduct. And I remember his hands, those beautiful hands, when he conducted. If I saw him in the street I would run after him so that he would notice me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came in once to our school to discuss music. And I remember he played a polonaise by Chopin, and taught us how the structure of music is, that there are sections of A section, B section, repeat section, C section. And he asked the class to identify, can we identify the various melodies? And I always raised my hand. I wanted not only to be good and-- and understand it, but I also wanted him to notice me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It was important to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but you understood a lot about music. That's what--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a lot. I started. That was my beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's what probably prompted you to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think he was very instrumental in my love also for music. It started at home, of course. But he took it-- he took it a step further. And then I studied piano. I started to-- to study piano with Mrs. Feigus, who was a very fine pianist. And her husband was a cellist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other schools that you attended? Were there any-- any subjects you learned besides music?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly what I learned. I really don't. But I'm sure that I did. I think I also-- actually I think I learned Yiddish. I learned to-- to read and write Yiddish. I think it was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't speak Yiddish before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes I did. Yes. Yes I did. But I don't remember how much I-- I wrote it or read it. Yes. We did-- now I remember-- we did learn about writers, about Sholem Aleichem, maybe and Sholem [? Marsh ?] or maybe Peretz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, mostly Jewish history.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, but I'm not sure. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said something about a theater. Were there any performances in this theater that you mentioned before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there was, but I have no memory of it. I-- I didn't attend any, I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, she wasn't going out to work. Only your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was. I think she was sewing. She was doing some things also. Because I think they used to bring uniforms to be-- to be made in the ghetto, soldiers' uniforms-- German soldiers' uniforms, I remember. But I don't remember what else she did. My aunt, which is my mother's sister, was for a while with us, but she didn't have a [NON ENGLISH] [? schein-- ?] an important Jew certificate. So my father arranged for her to be hidden in various places with Christian people who, after a while, were ready to report her. And she kept running and running from place to place. And then finally she, at the end, she did wind up at the ghetto with us. I think I remember that my father's colleagues did offer to take me, because my father's colleagues from-- from the plant were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Christian-- Christian people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they offered to take me. But I am told-- and I don't remember this myself-- that I threw plenty of tantrums not wanting to go. I just wanted to die. I didn't care. Not that I knew what death was, but I just didn't want to part from my parents, at least until I die. So I stayed with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then when you went to this school, were there any people who composed any music in that school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about the ghetto, let's say, or things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that anything that you can recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually one of the students at the conservatory, who was much more advanced than I was, he already played Bach and Beethoven and Mendelssohn. He composed, he wrote, the music to a song called 'Shtiler, Shtiler, Lomir Shvaygn 'Silence, Silence, Let's be Quiet', or let's be silent. It's about Ponary. And Kaczerginski, he was not only a lyricist, but he was also choreographer, and a very talented person. He wrote the lyrics. And actually I would-- I arranged it on the guitar so that I could introduce you to the melody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the lyrics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also translated one verse in English so that people who are not Yiddish speaking can at least understand what is happening. It's not the whole song, it's just one verse of Yiddish, and one verse in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please show, play it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mhm. [DRINKS] [SINGING IN YIDDISH] [SINGING] Silence silence, silence, silence. The Earth still simmers here. The sounds and cries of bitter moments row slowly in the air. There were roads that led to Ponar, closed to return. The forest watched a biggest horror, and no one even cared. Silence, dearest child of mine, it doesn't help to cry. The help we need is not in sight, they wouldn't even try. Freedom gates will one day open. We will show them, we will show them that we will survive. We will survive. We will survive. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for this lovely song.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the songs that was a sad song. But there were some other songs that were lighter and much more romantic. And there were people, of course, that-- I was not of romantic age yet. But there were some people who were. And sometimes they lost each other. And based on that emotion, another-- I later wrote my own song, which I'll perform later. But this is a tango in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was also the words, the same lyrics. The lyrics were written by Kaczerginski, the same poet. And the music is by Avrom Brudno. And it's a tango. And it also talks about traveling through the little streets of the ghetto. And this woman is-- is wondering about where her loved one disappeared. And she still remembers the place where she used to pass, and see him, and they would meet. And again, I translated one little verse to just give you an idea how she laments about the person that she had lost and no longer sees. [DRINKS] [SINGING IN YIDDISH] [SINGING] Returning from work I see your reflection. It's here that you greeted me there. It's here on this corner you waited and smiled. We kissed very gently in love. [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you ever so much for this lovely song.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is definitely a ghetto song about the streets, and people who knew each other and lost each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, when you were there in-- in this Vilna Ghetto, do you have anything like to show me where it was? Like a picture of it or something? Like a gate to the ghetto, or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. This is actually a cover of a-- of a recording. And all-- the two songs that I just performed are in this production. And this is a typical ghetto street that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the gate looked pretty much like this. But it would maybe have been someplace over here. And I love this record. This is how I learned these songs. And I, you know, arranged it in my key for the piano. I sing. I'm not particularly a singer.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was lovely. Thank you so much. Now, you can you tell me, did you have many friends there in the ghetto? I mean your girlfriends that you grew up with, or somebody you met there in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had some, yes. But I-- I don't have anybody from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, everybody's dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody's dead. I don't even remember people too well, except for one person who was my friend, because we lived in the same apartment-- in the same room rather. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last time I saw her was at the liquidation of ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Itele.

speaker: interviewer
text: Itele.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was very important to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lost her there in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost her. Well, I lost her at the liquidation of ghetto at the gate when-- when we were about to leave the ghetto forever. We all stood in a row, and she stood not far from me, just a few feet away with her parents. And I was there with my parents. My grandmother was already separated. And she was led with the elderly and children and mothers who had babies-- infants-- in their hand. They were completely separated from the older, or so to speak medium-range people. And [SOB] they were never heard of again. And that's how you lost your friend Itele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the SS came over to me. And he said-- [GERMAN] You little one. What are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] You have to go with the children. And my mother knew it was coming. So she already had prepared me. I had long braids. She like, rolled around, and put lipstick on me. I don't know. She later told me they-- they put me on something to make me taller because I'm not a short-- I'm not a tall person. I'm only five feet tall now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he-- he for some sort of reason, lost track of me. But he did take my girlfriend who was actually a little younger than I was, maybe a year or so. And she knew where she was going. You mean at that point, everybody knew what's going on in Ponary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that we were being separated from our parents. We already had heard-- at that point, we knew about Ponary. We knew already about [? Klug. ?] And we knew, I think, about Majdanek also. And at that point, we knew that it's certainly not a route to life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was taken away from her mother. And that moment is-- is deeply engraved in my mind, when she took off her little knapsack and she gave it to her mother and she said, here mommy. Here is my little knapsack. And don't forget that you had a little girl called Itele. What a terrible memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I will never forget that-- that moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what happened during your stay there in the ghetto? I don't want to distract from this moving story. But what were you doing when they were looking for you? Did you, perhaps, have an idea that you can hide somewhere or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father got together with a few colleagues of his. And they created a bunker, like a hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bunker is like a hiding place. And they-- it was like you had to go into the ground where the sewers are. You had to go in, really, in a hole. I was even afraid to slide in through the hole. I mean, I was smaller. But there are people who were larger than me and they had to slide through that hole. And you came down through-- through ladders and steps and whatever. And then there was like a big chamber room where they put, like, bunk beds. And there was one chimney that apparently supplied the air for that particular chamber. And while I was resting during a hiding episode--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went there. When did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the end, your yellow [NON ENGLISH] [? schein ?] didn't matter anymore, because they were just catching everybody. They were liquidating. They were about to destroy the Vilna Ghetto and its population.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this about? Do you recall? Was it a year or so that you spent there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was already in 1943. This was at the end. They just, they knew that Jews were hiding. And a lot of Jews created bunkers-- hiding places. So they used to just throw in bombs and explode the buildings. Many people-- I mean, I watched them bring out many dead bodies from the collapsed buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were mentioning that you were sleeping once and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was resting on one of those bunk beds. And I overheard my father speak to his colleagues that if we have to stay there more than two days, that we will all suffocate because there won't be a supply of air, which gave me plenty of scarring for the rest of my life about not having air to breathe, you know? At the same time, I also want to mention that my-- my father made me a ring. This is the ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the ring that he made you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the little ring that he made me from a silver coin. It was my first piece of jewelry. He made it for my-- he was not a jeweler but he-- he just made it. And it's made out of silver. And it has an interesting thing on it. It has a triangle, a vav, and a gimmel. The vav stands for Vilna, and the gimmel stands for ghetto-- Vilna Ghetto. And inside is the day when the ghetto was created.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was-- can you read it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was-- well, actually, this was in September. The 6th of September, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when they created the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Based on that ring I did artwork which I will show you later-- it's on the piano actually-- that is presently in a museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you manage to-- to keep hold-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the concentration camp, you were not allowed to have anything. I risked my life for this ring. What I used to do is like put it here in my crevasse like this and walk around like this whenever there was somebody in sight. I would just hold it, hide it over here if we passed someplace. And I made sure that-- that nobody sees it. Of course, I risked my life. If they had found me with this ring, I don't know what they would have done. I don't think I would have been alive today. As it is, they were-- you were always at risk. Every moment was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after that in the bunker? Did you ever use the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bunker, we actually used once or twice, and survived that cleaning, so to speak, the [NON-ENGLISH]. We did. And then it-- it toned down. And then-- you never knew when-- when it's coming. But somehow my father, I guess my father knew when it's about to happen, because the Jews, there were some leaders in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. Yeah. There were some.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not aware of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm aware of it. But I don't know who was in charge of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that there was a-- I think there was a Jew called [? Genz. ?] And he was the leader. I don't think he survived. I think it was a question of giving himself up, instead of another person that they had captured. And I think he-- he went to his death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying about your father, that he found out somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow the-- the leader would know that it's coming now. And at the end we knew because they were just throwing-- they were shelling and throwing bombs into these buildings. They wanted all the Jews to-- to come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at the very, very end when they liquidated the ghetto, we hid some place in-- in an attic. I don't even know whose attic it was, or where it was. I think it was a building that connected from the ghetto to the outside. And for some reason my father brought us up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we heard the Germans in the courtyard. And we knew that they are going to find us. And apparently they knew these little hiding places, that it's possible to hide there in the attic. And we heard them clearly coming. And I-- when I saw them they were all standing there with the pistols and with the machine guns. And I knew those were my last moments. I just didn't. They took us out from there. We were standing in a yard. And they were walking around with the pistols. And I thought that any second it's going to be the end of us. But somehow they didn't. They brought us to the gate where the rest of the people from Vilna were, whoever they gathered. And they didn't shoot us. But that was the last time I saw my father. And my brother ran away at that point. But they also caught him, and he wound up in another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what happened to your father? You mentioned that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- was brought in. They separated us. And he was taken with the men. And we didn't know where my father was until a- many months later. Actually-- [SIGHS] Yeah, many months later. We didn't know if my father was even alive. But then we found that he was in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the rest of you, the women and the children, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took us to a train. And we rode that train for days and days with no water, and no food, and no toilets--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you in that-- was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --packed like sheep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Covered wagon or open wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, like-- like sheep would come in a-- in that type of wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't a regular train with seats or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no. No. We were all on the floor. No. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a lot of you, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were packed. Packed. We thought we were going to Ponar. Again, we thought we were going to Ponar. My brother jumped out of the window from the train going-- which he thought going to Ponary. And my-- my mother gave each of us some-- in our clothes-- some gold. And he somehow went into the woods. They were shooting after him, he tells me, he told me. And he wound up in-- in some kind of hiding that my father knew. But these were all temporary things. You know, nobody, no Christian, very few Christians were about to risk their life. Because you know, they were-- if they caught a Christian hiding a Jew they would shoot them on the spot. But they did maybe let him stay overnight and gave him some food, until he went to a further destination. And then he wound up in a different place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your mother remained alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and I, and my Aunt Frida. She-- the three of us were in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the lady who lived with you you mentioned before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was my-- it's like my surrogate mother. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did survive the war. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in the train for at least a week. We didn't know where we were. We thought that we are going to Ponary. Because many times the train just stopped and didn't move and we didn't know where we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any food with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. No. We were hungry. We didn't have toilets. It was terrible. And the mother, Itele's mother, was sitting there and hallucinating. I still remember that scene. She was talking to Itele. Itele, mein kind, Itele, my child, you know? She was totally in a state of schizophrenia. Reality escaped her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She lost-- this little girl was her--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was her only child. Yes. So [SIGHS] finally we arrived to a place called Riga. It was a [NON-ENGLISH] It was like a lager type of thing. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a vernichtungslager? In other words, were they killing the people that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember. I remember that we were afraid to go into the showers, because by now, we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew already about the showers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We knew about the showers. Yes. They, of course, shaved our heads and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. And were there any other people who were taking part in this terrible thing with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans only? Or was there other--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians. They were all criminals. They were walking around with whips in their hands, and would constantly-- it's like-- like for them it was a game, you know? Constantly whip anybody who was in sight. And there it was a whole different setting. We were like about 700 people in one barrack, and sleeping four in a bed. You know, like four in a bed. It wasn't a bed. It was like a little, tiny 30 inch--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a bunk bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: --wide sleeping quarter, maybe three levels or so. Like a typical barrack type of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a bin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a barrack, a typical barrack, you know, of a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said that when you arrived, they took you to showers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us those clothes, you know, with the lines. And we had to pass through the streets-- you know, I mean, not the streets. It was inside the camp-- naked or not naked. It didn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any civilians there? I mean like people who lived there, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was just a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no. This was a definitely a segregated area.

speaker: interviewer
text: A camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was wired with electrical wires. This was a camp. There were no houses. No. There were some blockaltestes who were the leaders of it.
